questions.question: Explain the position of Data Link Layer in OSI and TCP-IP models. Mention its primary responsibility.
keyPoints: DLL is Layer 2 in OSI model, between Physical and Network layers
In TCP-IP, it corresponds to Link/Network Interface layer
Primary responsibility: node-to-node reliable data transfer
Transforms raw bits into meaningful data frames

questions.question: List and briefly explain the four main services provided by Data Link Layer.
keyPoints: Framing: encapsulates network layer data into frames with headers/trailers
Error Control: detects and sometimes corrects transmission errors
Flow Control: prevents fast sender from overwhelming slow receiver
Access Control: coordinates access to shared media (MAC protocols)

questions.question: Differentiate between single-bit errors and burst errors with examples.
keyPoints: Single-bit error: only one bit changes (e.g., 01010101 → 01010111)
Burst error: multiple consecutive bits corrupted (e.g., 10 consecutive bit errors)
Burst errors more common in communication channels due to noise bursts
Burst length measured from first to last error bit

questions.question: Compare three error detection methods: Parity Check, Checksum, and CRC.
keyPoints: Parity Check: simplest, adds 1 bit for even/odd parity, detects odd number of errors
Checksum: groups data into words, sums them, sends complement - better coverage
CRC: uses polynomial division, most powerful, detects all burst errors up to degree n
Effectiveness increases: Parity < Checksum < CRC

questions.question: What is Hamming Code? Explain the relationship between data bits (m) and parity bits (p) in Hamming code.
keyPoints: Hamming code is a forward error correction (FEC) method
Uses redundant parity bits to not only detect but also locate single-bit errors
Formula: 2^p >= m + p + 1 (Hamming inequality)
Parity bits placed at positions 1,2,4,8... (powers of 2)

questions.question: Explain the sub-layers of Data Link Layer: LLC and MAC. What functions does each perform?
keyPoints: LLC (Logical Link Control): Upper sub-layer, provides multiplexing, flow control, error handling
MAC (Media Access Control): Lower sub-layer, handles addressing, channel access, collision handling
LLC is protocol-independent, MAC is hardware-specific
Together they separate logical and physical addressing concerns

questions.question: With the help of a suitable example, explain how Hamming code can detect and correct a single-bit error. Show the arrangement of data bits and parity bits.
keyPoints: Explain Hamming code structure with positions numbered 1 to n
Parity bits at positions 1,2,4,8... (2^k powers)
Data bits at remaining positions
For error detection: check each parity bit covering specific bit positions
If parity mismatch found, calculate sum of parity positions with error - this gives error bit position
Correct by flipping the erroneous bit
Example with 4 data bits (m=4) requires p=3 parity bits (2^3=8 >= 4+3+1=8)

questions.question: Explain the concept of CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) with an example. How is it more effective than simple parity checking?
keyPoints: CRC uses polynomial division concept
Sender and receiver agree on a generator polynomial G(x)
Data bits treated as polynomial coefficients, divided by G(x)
Remainder (CRC bits) appended to data
Receiver divides received frame by same G(x), remainder should be zero
Detects all single-bit errors, all burst errors <= degree of polynomial
Simple parity can only detect odd number of bit errors; CRC much more robust
In practice, CRC-32 used for Ethernet, CRC-16 for serial communications

questions.question: Describe the error control mechanisms at Data Link Layer. Differentiate between error detection and error correction with examples of when each is used.
keyPoints: Error Detection: ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest) - stop-and-wait ARQ, go-back-N, selective repeat
Error Correction: FEC (Forward Error Correction) - Hamming code, Reed-Solomon codes
Error Detection uses: parity, checksum, CRC - adds redundant bits to detect errors
Error Correction uses: adds more redundancy to locate AND fix errors
Detection + Retransmission (ARQ): used in reliable channels with feedback (TCP, link protocols)
FEC: used in unreliable channels, one-way communication (satellite TV, deep space communication)
Trade-off: FEC adds more overhead but avoids retransmission delays
